blocks.statement: I can interpret electric field lines (out of + charges, into − charges, denser = stronger), use E = F/q to compute force on a charge in a known field, and explain why a NEUTRAL object can still feel a force in an electric field (induced polarization).

blocks.whatWeKnow: Coulomb's law tells us the force between charges. But what if there are MANY charges, or a charge in empty space?
blocks.connection: Field is the answer to 'how does one charge KNOW about another at a distance?' It's a property of every point in space — what a + test charge would feel.

blocks.markdown:
## Force per coulomb

Coulomb's law handles two charges at a time. But space around a charge is already 'primed' — drop a test charge anywhere and it feels a push. That readiness-to-push is the **electric field**:

**E = F / q** — the force PER UNIT of positive charge, in N/C.

Flip it around and it becomes a tool: if you know the field, the force on any charge q is **F = E·q**. Positive charges get pushed ALONG the field; negative charges get pushed AGAINST it.

We draw fields with **field lines**: out of + charges, into − charges, denser where the field is stronger. And for a single point charge, the field strength at distance r is **E = kq/r²** — Coulomb's law with one charge factored out.

Today also pays off Day 1's mystery: why did NEUTRAL paper bits jump to the balloon? The field **polarizes** them — rearranges their internal charges — and the nearer side wins the tug-of-war.

terms.definition: A vector quantity at every point in space. E = F/q — the force per unit positive charge. Units: N/C (or V/m). In a uniform field E = 100 N/C, a +2 C charge feels F = E·q = 200 N in the direction of E.
terms.cognate: Sp. campo eléctrico · Pt. campo elétrico · HC chan elektrik

terms.definition: Drawn arrows showing E direction at every point. Lines come OUT of + charges, INTO − charges. Denser lines = stronger field. A single + charge: field lines radiate outward like spokes. A single − charge: lines point inward. Between + and −: lines arc from + to −.
terms.cognate: Sp. líneas de campo · Pt. linhas de campo · HC liy chan

terms.definition: A neutral object placed in an electric field rearranges its own charges — the + side faces toward the − source, and vice versa. The object becomes a tiny dipole. The balloon attracts neutral paper bits because the field POLARIZES the paper: the closer (+) end feels stronger attraction than the (−) end feels repulsion → net pull toward the balloon.
terms.cognate: Sp. polarización · Pt. polarização · HC polarizasyon

blocks.title: Field lines are a DRAWING TOOL — and E doesn't depend on the test charge
blocks.markdown: There aren't actually 'lines' in space — the field exists at EVERY point. And E is a property of the SOURCE charges, independent of the test charge; the test charge just tells you what HAPPENS in the field.

blocks.instruction: Sketch field lines for: (A) a single + charge alone, (B) a single − charge alone, (C) a + and − together (dipole). Where are the lines most dense? Where is E strongest?
prompts: Three panels: A (+ alone), B (− alone), C (dipole).
Circle where the lines are densest (strongest E).

blocks.prompt: FORCE ON A CHARGE IN A KNOWN FIELD — An electric field E = 50 N/C points to the right (+x). What force does it exert on a +3 C charge placed in it?
blocks.answer: F = 150 N pointing right (same direction as E, because q is positive).

blocks.prompt: FORCE ON A NEGATIVE CHARGE IN THE SAME FIELD — Same field E = 50 N/C in +x. Now place a −2 C charge in it. Compute the force.
blocks.work:
F = 50 · (−2)
F = −100 N (in +x direction)
F = 100 N pointing in −x (LEFT)
blocks.answer: F = 100 N to the LEFT. Negative charges feel force OPPOSITE to E.

blocks.prompt: YOUR TURN — FIELD FROM A + CHARGE. A +5 μC point charge is fixed at the origin. What's the electric field magnitude at a point 0.10 m away? (E = kq/r², for a point source.)
blocks.equationHint: E = kq/r² — one charge, so no q₂. Expect a few million N/C.

blocks.title: Day 1's mystery, solved
blocks.markdown: The paper bits leaping to the balloon: the balloon's negative charge creates an E field. The neutral paper polarizes — its + side closer to the balloon — so the attractive force on the + side wins. NEUTRAL objects feel forces in NON-UNIFORM fields.

blocks.prompt: An electric field E = 100 N/C points east. A −1 μC charge sits in the field. What force does it feel? (Magnitude AND direction.) Show one line of work. Then complete the frame.
blocks.frame: Electric field lines come OUT of ___ charges, IN to ___ charges. The force on a charge q is F = ___. For a positive q, F points along ___; for a negative q, F points ___.

blocks.title: Read & practice
blocks.markdown: Open the reader and read **33.1–33.3** (electric fields, field lines, and shielding). Answer the questions beside the reading as you go.

blocks.title: Electric Fields and Potential — read & practice
